 {"id":"bd-130l","title":"Responsive Layout Breakpoints System","description":"## Background

Terminals vary wildly in size:
- Phone SSH client: 40x20
- Standard terminal: 80x24
- Modern laptop: 120x40
- 4K monitor: 200x60+

Currently, widgets either overflow or waste space. Dashboard could show 1 column on small, 3 columns on large.

## Solution: Responsive Layout System

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum Breakpoint {
    XSmall,  // < 60 cols
    Small,   // 60-79 cols
    Medium,  // 80-119 cols
    Large,   // 120-159 cols
    XLarge,  // 160+ cols
}

impl Breakpoint {
    pub fn from_width(width: u16) -> Self {
        match width {
            0..60 => Self::XSmall,
            60..80 => Self::Small,
            80..120 => Self::Medium,
            120..160 => Self::Large,
            _ => Self::XLarge,
        }
    }
    
    pub fn is_at_least(&self, other: Self) -> bool;
    pub fn is_at_most(&self, other: Self) -> bool;
}

/// Responsive value that changes with breakpoint
pub struct Responsive<T> {
    xsmall: T,
    small: Option<T>,
    medium: Option<T>,
    large: Option<T>,
    xlarge: Option<T>,
}

impl<T: Clone> Responsive<T> {
    pub fn get(&self, bp: Breakpoint) -> &T;
}

// Usage
let columns = Responsive::new(1)  // XSmall: 1 column
    .medium(2)                     // Medium: 2 columns
    .large(3);                     // Large: 3 columns

let layout = ResponsiveLayout::new()
    .xsmall(|area| Layout::vertical([...]))
    .medium(|area| Layout::horizontal([...]));
```

## Integration with Layout Crate

```rust
// In ftui-layout
pub struct Layout {
    // ... existing fields
    responsive: Option<Box<dyn Fn(Breakpoint, Rect) -> Layout>>,
}

impl Layout {
    pub fn responsive<F>(f: F) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(Breakpoint, Rect) -> Layout + 'static;
}
```

## Benefits

- Optimal UX on any terminal size
- CSS-like mental model
- Progressive enhancement

## Files to Create

- crates/ftui-layout/src/responsive.rs
- crates/ftui-layout/src/breakpoint.rs

## Success Criteria
- Epic delivers a cohesive user-visible capability: Responsive Layout Breakpoints System
- Child tasks are not just implemented; each has unit tests + E2E scripts with verbose JSONL logs and deterministic fixtures
- Graceful degradation tiers are specified (capabilities, fallbacks, safety) and validated in tests
- Formal invariants documented + enforced via property tests; any dynamic decisions have an evidence ledger and debug UI


## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.
- Layout: formulate constraints explicitly; consider LP/dual sensitivity for responsive variants; enforce stable tie-breaking for determinism.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.
- Metamorphic tests: shrink/grow, reorder non-semantic inputs, and ensure invariants still hold.

 {"id":"bd-1ho.7","title":"Implement logging integration","description":"# Implement logging integration (rich-style formatting on top of tracing)

## Objective
Provide an optional logging layer that formats `tracing` events into styled output (Segments) in a way that is:
- readable for humans
- deterministic for tests
- compatible with ftui’s one-writer routing

## Reference (for ideas)
- `legacy_reference_library_code/rich_rust/src/logging.rs`

## Scope
- A formatter/subscriber (or layer) that:
  - applies per-level styling (error/warn/info/debug/trace)
  - supports timestamps (configurable)
  - optionally shows source location (file:line)
  - formats structured fields consistently

## One-Writer Rule (Critical)
- This integration must not write directly to stdout/stderr in a way that can interleave with ftui rendering.
- Output should be routed into a sink that can be:
  - captured for tests, or
  - forwarded via ftui’s `LogSink`/TerminalWriter policy.

## Tests Required
- Unit tests for formatting choices (stable snapshots of formatted lines/segments).
- Concurrency test: multiple threads logging cannot interleave within a single formatted record.
- Integration test (optional) that shows logs routed into the harness log region.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A tracing integration exists (subscriber/layer) with configurable formatting.
- [ ] Output is Segment-based and can be captured deterministically in tests.
- [ ] One-writer routing is respected (no hidden direct terminal writes).

## E2E Test Scenarios

### Console Pipeline E2E
```rust
#[test]
fn e2e_console_full_render() {
    // Setup
    let mut console = Console::with_width(80);
    console.begin_capture();
    
    // Render complex content
    console.rule(Some("Header"));
    console.print(Panel::new(Text::from("Content")).with_title("Box"));
    console.print(Columns::new(vec![col1, col2]));
    console.rule(None);
    
    // Verify
    let segments = console.end_capture();
    assert!(!segments.is_empty());
    assert!(segments.iter().any(|s| s.text.contains("Header")));
    // ... more assertions
}
```

### Measurement Chain E2E
```rust
#[test]
fn e2e_measurement_chain() {
    let content = Group::new(vec![
        Box::new(Padding::new(
            Panel::new(Text::from("Inner")),
            Sides::all(2)
        )),
        Box::new(Rule::new()),
    ]);
    
    let measurement = content.measure(&console, 80);
    assert!(measurement.minimum <= measurement.maximum);
    assert!(measurement.maximum <= 80);
    
    let rendered = content.render(&console, 80);
    for line in &rendered.lines {
        let width: usize = line.iter().map(|s| s.cell_length()).sum();
        assert!(width <= 80);
    }
}
```

## Logging Requirements
- `tracing::info!` for console output operations
- `tracing::debug!` for measurement calculations
- `tracing::trace!` for segment construction
- Live display refreshes logged at trace level

## Coverage Targets
- Line coverage: >= 90%
- Branch coverage: >= 85%

Dependencies: All Rich Core modules, Testing infrastructure (bd-10i.11)


## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-1i67","title":"Animation Choreographer","description":"## Overview
Provide high-level animation composition system for coordinating complex multi-element animations.

## Rationale
- Individual animations are simple but coordination is hard
- Staggered, parallel, and sequential timing needed
- Animation groups should share lifecycle
- Enables polished onboarding and transitions

## Core Components
1. **Timeline**: Sequence of animation events with timing
2. **Animation Group**: Multiple animations with shared control
3. **Stagger**: Apply delay between group members
4. **Callbacks**: Events at animation milestones
5. **Presets**: Common patterns (fade-in-group, cascade, etc.)

## Implementation Strategy
- Build on existing Animation types in ftui-widgets
- Create Choreographer in ftui-extras
- Integrate with Tick events from ftui-runtime
- Provide builder API for timeline construction

## Usage Example
Choreographer::new()
    .add(fade_in(header), Duration::ZERO)
    .add_staggered(items.map(slide_in), ms(50))
    .on_complete(|| show_content())
    .start()

## Preset Animations
- cascade_in: Staggered entrance from top
- fan_out: Elements spread from center
- typewriter: Character-by-character reveal
- pulse_sequence: Attention-grabbing highlight

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for timeline calculation
- Snapshot tests at key animation frames
- Integration tests for callback timing
- Performance tests for large animation groups

## Success Criteria
- Complex animations can be composed declaratively
- Animations stay synchronized across elements
- Preset library covers common use cases

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.

 {"id":"bd-1i67.1","title":"Timeline Data Structure","description":"Sequence of animation events with timing.

## Requirements
- Timeline with keyed events
- Absolute and relative timing
- Loop and repeat support
- Pause/resume/seek control

## Proposed API

```rust
pub struct Timeline<M> {
    events: Vec<TimelineEvent<M>>,
    duration: Duration,
    loop_count: LoopCount,
    state: PlaybackState,
    current_time: Duration,
}

pub struct TimelineEvent<M> {
    pub offset: Duration,       // When event starts
    pub duration: Duration,     // How long it runs
    pub animation: Box<dyn Animation<M>>,
    pub label: Option<String>,  // For seeking by name
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
pub enum LoopCount {
    Once,
    Times(u32),
    Infinite,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
pub enum PlaybackState {
    Idle,
    Playing,
    Paused,
    Finished,
}

impl<M> Timeline<M> {
    pub fn new() -> Self;
    
    /// Add event at absolute offset
    pub fn add(self, offset: Duration, anim: impl Animation<M>) -> Self;
    
    /// Add event relative to last event's end
    pub fn then(self, anim: impl Animation<M>) -> Self;
    
    /// Add event with label
    pub fn add_labeled(self, label: &str, offset: Duration, anim: impl Animation<M>) -> Self;
    
    /// Set loop behavior
    pub fn loop_count(self, count: LoopCount) -> Self;
    
    /// Playback control
    pub fn play(&mut self);
    pub fn pause(&mut self);
    pub fn resume(&mut self);
    pub fn stop(&mut self);
    pub fn seek(&mut self, time: Duration);
    pub fn seek_label(&mut self, label: &str);
    
    /// Process tick, return messages
    pub fn tick(&mut self, delta: Duration) -> Vec<M>;
    
    /// Current progress 0.0 - 1.0
    pub fn progress(&self) -> f32;
    
    /// Check if complete
    pub fn is_finished(&self) -> bool;
}
```

## Implementation Details

### Event Scheduling
Store events sorted by offset. On each tick:
1. Advance current_time by delta
2. Find events that should be active (offset <= current_time < offset + duration)
3. Call tick() on each active animation
4. Collect messages

### Looping
When current_time >= duration and loop_count allows:
- Reset current_time to 0
- Decrement loop counter (if finite)
- Reset all animations

## File: crates/ftui-extras/src/animation/timeline.rs

## Unit Tests
- test_sequential_events: A then B then C
- test_overlapping_events: Parallel animations
- test_loop_finite: Loops exact count
- test_loop_infinite: Never finishes
- test_seek_time: Jump to specific time
- test_seek_label: Jump to labeled event
- test_pause_resume: State preservation

 {"id":"bd-1nn0","title":"Add recovery mechanism with configurable attempts","description":"# Add recovery mechanism with configurable attempts

## Background
Part of Widget Error Boundaries (bd-1euu). After a widget fails, we should try to recover when conditions change (e.g., model updates).

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Configuration for error boundary behavior
#[derive(Clone)]
pub struct ErrorBoundaryConfig {
    /// Maximum recovery attempts before giving up
    pub max_recovery_attempts: u32,
    
    /// Delay between recovery attempts (in frames)
    pub recovery_delay_frames: u32,
    
    /// Whether to log errors
    pub log_errors: bool,
    
    /// Custom fallback widget factory
    pub fallback_factory: Option<Box<dyn Fn(&CapturedError) -> Box<dyn Widget>>>,
    
    /// Whether to try recovery on model update
    pub recover_on_model_update: bool,
}

impl Default for ErrorBoundaryConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_recovery_attempts: 3,
            recovery_delay_frames: 5,
            log_errors: true,
            fallback_factory: None,
            recover_on_model_update: true,
        }
    }
}

impl<W: Widget> ErrorBoundary<W> {
    /// Attempt to recover from error state
    /// Call this when model changes or after delay
    pub fn try_recover(&mut self) -> bool {
        match &mut self.state {
            ErrorBoundaryState::Failed(error) => {
                if self.config.max_recovery_attempts > 0 {
                    tracing::info!(
                        widget = self.name,
                        attempt = 1,
                        "Starting recovery attempt"
                    );
                    self.state = ErrorBoundaryState::Recovering {
                        attempts: 1,
                        last_error: error.clone(),
                    };
                    true
                } else {
                    false
                }
            }
            ErrorBoundaryState::Recovering { attempts, last_error } => {
                if *attempts < self.config.max_recovery_attempts {
                    *attempts += 1;
                    tracing::info!(
                        widget = self.name,
                        attempt = *attempts,
                        max = self.config.max_recovery_attempts,
                        "Continuing recovery attempt"
                    );
                    true
                } else {
                    // Give up permanently
                    self.state = ErrorBoundaryState::Failed(last_error.clone());
                    tracing::warn!(
                        widget = self.name,
                        "Recovery failed after max attempts"
                    );
                    false
                }
            }
            ErrorBoundaryState::Healthy => true,
        }
    }
    
    /// Reset to healthy state (force recovery)
    pub fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.state = ErrorBoundaryState::Healthy;
        tracing::info!(widget = self.name, "Error boundary reset");
    }
    
    /// Signal that model has updated (may trigger recovery)
    pub fn on_model_update(&mut self) {
        if self.config.recover_on_model_update && self.is_failed() {
            self.try_recover();
        }
    }
    
    /// Get recovery status
    pub fn recovery_status(&self) -> RecoveryStatus {
        match &self.state {
            ErrorBoundaryState::Healthy => RecoveryStatus::Healthy,
            ErrorBoundaryState::Failed(_) => RecoveryStatus::Failed,
            ErrorBoundaryState::Recovering { attempts, .. } => {
                RecoveryStatus::Recovering {
                    attempts: *attempts,
                    max_attempts: self.config.max_recovery_attempts,
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

/// Recovery status for external monitoring
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RecoveryStatus {
    Healthy,
    Failed,
    Recovering { attempts: u32, max_attempts: u32 },
}
```

## Recovery Triggers
1. **Manual**: Call try_recover() explicitly
2. **Model update**: Automatic when config.recover_on_model_update
3. **Force reset**: Call reset() to immediately clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ErrorBoundaryConfig with all options
- [ ] try_recover() increments attempts
- [ ] Max attempts enforced
- [ ] reset() forces healthy state
- [ ] on_model_update() triggers recovery
- [ ] RecoveryStatus for monitoring","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:18:18.791599230Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T19:29:50.166964103Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T19:29:50.166938815Z","close_reason":"Recovery mechanism already implemented in ErrorBoundaryState with try_recover(max_attempts), Recovering state, attempts counter, and configurable max_recovery_attempts","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1nn0","depends_on_id":"bd-1fpo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T14:32:17Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

- DA1 (CSI c / CSI 0 c): Primary device attributes — many CLI programs send this as a startup handshake

- G0 designate (ESC ( B / ESC ( 0): ASCII / DEC Special Graphics

- SS2 (ESC N): Single shift to G2
- SS3 (ESC O): Single shift to G3

 {"id":"bd-1oz","title":"Implement LRU width cache for text measurement","description":"# Implement LRU Width Cache for Text Measurement

## Background
Plan Section 1.4 and Appendix D.3 explicitly mention the "LRU width cache" from rich_rust as a key optimization. Text width measurement is a hot path (called for every render) and must be cached.

## Why LRU Cache?
- Unicode width calculation is expensive (grapheme segmentation + width lookup)
- Same text strings appear repeatedly (labels, status text, etc.)
- Cache hit rate expected to be high for typical UIs
- Prevents O(n) per-frame width recalculation

## Design
```rust
/// Width cache with LRU eviction
pub struct WidthCache {
    cache: LinkedHashMap<u64, usize>,  // hash -> width
    capacity: usize,
}

impl WidthCache {
    pub fn new(capacity: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            cache: LinkedHashMap::with_capacity(capacity),
            capacity,
        }
    }

    /// Get cached width or compute and cache
    pub fn get_or_compute(&mut self, text: &str, compute: impl FnOnce() -> usize) -> usize {
        let hash = hash_text(text);
        
        if let Some(&width) = self.cache.get(&hash) {
            // Move to front (LRU refresh)
            self.cache.get_refresh(&hash);
            return width;
        }
        
        let width = compute();
        
        // Evict if over capacity
        while self.cache.len() >= self.capacity {
            self.cache.pop_front();
        }
        
        self.cache.insert(hash, width);
        width
    }
    
    pub fn clear(&mut self) {
        self.cache.clear();
    }
}

// Use FNV or xxhash for fast hashing
fn hash_text(text: &str) -> u64 {
    use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
    let mut hasher = FnvHasher::default();
    text.hash(&mut hasher);
    hasher.finish()
}
```

## Integration Points
- `RenderContext` holds a `RefCell<WidthCache>`
- `Text::cell_length()` uses the cache
- `Segment::cell_length()` uses the cache
- Cache is cleared on theme change (fonts can affect width)

## Cache Sizing
- Default: 1024 entries (covers typical UI with headroom)
- Configurable via `WidthCache::new(capacity)`
- Memory footprint: ~32 bytes per entry (hash + width + list pointers)

## Performance Requirements
- Cache hit: < 50ns (hash + lookup)
- Cache miss: < 5µs (grapheme segmentation + width calculation)
- Hash collision handling: rare, just recompute

## Tests Required
- [ ] Cache hits return correct cached value
- [ ] Cache misses compute and cache
- [ ] LRU eviction works correctly
- [ ] Clear() empties cache
- [ ] Thread safety if needed (RefCell for single-threaded)
- [ ] Performance benchmarks

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LRU cache implemented with configurable capacity
- [ ] Integrated into text measurement hot path
- [ ] Performance benchmarks show significant speedup
- [ ] No correctness regressions in width measurement

 {"id":"bd-1plj","title":"Async Task Manager E2E: Anytime-Valid Flake Detector (e-process)","description":"## Goal
Detect flaky timing regressions in E2E tests without inflating false positives, using anytime-valid statistics.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Compute an e-value for latency/outcome deviations during E2E runs. For sub-Gaussian residuals r_t (mean 0 under null), use:
- e_t = exp(lambda * r_t - (lambda^2 * sigma^2) / 2)
- E_t = product_{i=1..t} e_i
Fail early only when E_t > 1/alpha, preserving validity under optional stopping.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Early fail only with strong evidence; no false alarms in stable runs.
- Works with variable-length test runs.
- Logs e-value trajectory and decision point.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_eprocess_threshold: failure at E_t > 1/alpha.
- unit_eprocess_nonnegative: e-values never negative.
- unit_optional_stopping: stopping early preserves decision validity (simulated).

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Inject synthetic latency spikes; verify early failure and log evidence.
- No spike: no failure; log shows stable e-values.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-1qb","title":"Implement Image widget with protocol detection","description":"PHASE 7 - EXTRAS: IMAGE WIDGET

## Background
Image widget displays images in terminal using Kitty Graphics Protocol, iTerm2 inline images, or Sixel fallback. Requires protocol detection and graceful degradation.

## Technical Requirements
- Protocol detection:
  - Kitty Graphics Protocol (XTGETTCAP query)
  - iTerm2 inline images (TERM_PROGRAM check)

  - Fallback: ASCII/Braille representation
- Image loading via image crate
- Resize/fit modes: Contain, Cover, Stretch, None
- Aspect ratio preservation
- Partial update for animated GIFs
- Memory management for large images

## Design Rationale
Image support is a premium feature gated behind 'image' flag. Protocol detection happens once at startup and caches result. Fallback ensures graceful degradation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Protocol detection and caching
- [ ] Kitty Graphics Protocol implementation
- [ ] iTerm2 inline image implementation
- [ ] Sixel fallback (optional sub-feature)
- [ ] ASCII fallback when no protocol available
- [ ] Resize mode configuration
- [ ] Aspect ratio handling
- [ ] Feature gate: image

## Performance Budget
- Protocol detection: < 50ms (one-time)
- Image encode/transmit: proportional to size

## Background
Sparkline and Chart widgets provide data visualization for metrics, trends, and timeseries. Common requirement for monitoring dashboards and CLI tools.

- Compact single-line visualization
- Data as Vec<f64> or iterator

- BarChart:

- LineChart:

  - Point markers optional

Feature-gated for projects that need visualization. Sparkline is minimal overhead. Full charts use Canvas internally for rendering.

5) Scale selection: derive total day span; choose tick unit (day/week/month) based on available width. Deterministic thresholds (e.g., if days<=width- label_col, unit=day; else if weeks<=width-..., unit=week; else month).
6) Layout: label column width = min(max label len, area.width/3) with ellipsis. Row height 1 per task, blank row per section. Axis row on top, optional section headers.

### Cross-Platform E2E Script

Create `scripts/cross_platform_e2e.sh`:
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Run on CI with matrix strategy

echo "Platform: $(uname -s)"
echo "Terminal: $TERM"

# Build
cargo build -p ftui-demo-showcase --release

# Run headless tests
cargo test -p ftui-core -- --nocapture
cargo test -p ftui-render -- --nocapture

# Snapshot tests (should be identical across platforms)
cargo test -p ftui-demo-showcase --test screen_snapshots
```

## CI Configuration

Add matrix builds to .github/workflows/ci.yml:
```yaml
cross-platform:
  strategy:
    matrix:
      os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
  runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly
    - run: cargo test -p ftui-core
    - run: cargo test -p ftui-render
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Linux tests passing
- [ ] macOS tests passing
- [ ] Windows tests passing
- [ ] CI matrix configured
- [ ] Platform-specific capability detection tested
- [ ] Snapshot tests identical across platforms
- [ ] docs/WINDOWS.md updated with validated status

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-3e1t.5.4","title":"Startup perf: lazy-init heavy demo screens","description":"Background
- Demo startup time is inflated by eager initialization of all screens and effect state.
- For reliable perf measurements (and better UX), we should lazily initialize heavy screens.

Goals
- Reduce startup overhead by deferring heavy screen state until first access.
- Keep behavior identical once a screen has been visited.

Scope / Work Items
- Identify heavy screens (Visual Effects, File Browser, Graph/Diagram demos, etc.).
- Convert ScreenStates to lazily initialized Option/OnceCell-like fields.
- Ensure initialization happens exactly once per screen and is logged with duration.
- Provide deterministic behavior: same screen produces same output after initialization.
- Add a quick log event on screen init: screen_id, init_ms, effect_count, memory_estimate (if available).

Constraints
- No output changes or quality reductions.
- No new files unless absolutely necessary.
- Keep per-screen init cheap and avoid any work when screen is not visited.

Tests / Validation
- Unit test: screen registry can resolve an uninitialized screen and initialize on first use.
- E2E run: start app, navigate to each heavy screen, and assert init logs exist once per screen.
- Ensure no regression in existing snapshot tests for screen output.

Notes
- This is a prerequisite for cleaner profiling and better perceived performance.","acceptance_criteria":"1) Startup path avoids constructing heavy screen states (verified by init logs).
